title: Listeria monocytogenes Infections
authors: Karen P. Neil, Jennifer P. Collins, Patricia M. Griffin

items.title: Understanding Listeria's Environmental Resilience and Pathogenic Mechanisms
items.content: Listeria monocytogenes is a gram-positive rod that thrives in various environments due to its ability to grow at low temperatures (4°C) and tolerate acidic, high-salt conditions. This resilience contributes to its transmission via contaminated food. Once ingested, Listeria uses internalins for cell invasion, listeriolysin O for vacuole escape, and ActA for intracellular movement, facilitating dissemination in the host.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes is a nonsporulating, facultatively anaerobic gram-positive rod.
Grows well on blood agar with small zones of $\beta$-hemolysis.
Can grow at refrigerator temperatures as low as 4°C.
Tolerates low-pH and high-salt environments.
Serotypes causing most human illness: 1/2a, 1/2b, and 4b.
Virulence factors include internalins (InlA, InlB), listeriolysin O (LLO), phospholipases, and ActA.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Risk Factors and Pathogenesis of Listeriosis in Vulnerable Populations
items.content: Invasive listeriosis primarily affects pregnant women, neonates, older adults, and immunocompromised individuals. The pathogen's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and placenta increases its risk for severe outcomes like meningoencephalitis. Factors such as increased gastric pH from proton pump inhibitors may enhance survival in the gastrointestinal tract, facilitating systemic dissemination.
keyPoints: Major risk groups: pregnant women, neonates, older adults, immunocompromised persons.
Invasive disease includes bacteremia and meningoencephalitis.
Increased gastric pH promotes Listeria's survival in the GI tract.
L. monocytogenes can cross the blood-brain barrier and placenta.
Iron-overload conditions like hemochromatosis are associated with listeriosis.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Why is invasive listeriosis associated with impaired cellular immunity?
items.content: Invasive listeriosis is linked to conditions that impair cellular immunity due to the reliance on both innate and adaptive immune responses for controlling L. monocytogenes infection. The key cytokines involved in the innate response are interferon $ γ $ and tumor necrosis factor $ α $ (TNF- $ α $), while cytotoxic CD8+ T cells drive long-term immunity.
keyPoints: Infection with L. monocytogenes is rare due to effective innate and adaptive immune responses.
Key cytokines in the innate response include interferon $ γ $ and TNF- $ α $.
Cytotoxic (CD8+) T cells are crucial for long-term immunity against L. monocytogenes.
Immunocompromising conditions, particularly those impairing cellular immunity, increase the risk of invasive listeriosis.
TNF- $ α $ inhibitors have been associated with increased reports of invasive listeriosis, leading to a boxed warning by the FDA.

items.title: How are L. monocytogenes strains used in cancer immunotherapy?
items.content: L. monocytogenes induces a strong cell-mediated immune response, which is leveraged in clinical trials using attenuated strains expressing foreign antigens as potential cancer immunotherapies.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes triggers a vigorous cell-mediated immune response.
Attenuated strains of L. monocytogenes that express foreign antigens are being tested for their potential in cancer immunotherapy.

items.title: Listeria monocytogenes: Foodborne Transmission and High-Risk Populations
items.content: L. monocytogenes is primarily transmitted through food, with ready-to-eat refrigerated foods being particularly concerning due to the organism's ability to grow at refrigerator temperatures. The incidence of invasive listeriosis in the U.S. was 2.1–3.3 cases per million persons from 2011-2022. High-risk groups include older adults (risk increases with age over 59), immunocompromised individuals, and pregnant women.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes transmission is almost always foodborne.
Incidence of invasive listeriosis: 2.1–3.3 cases per million persons during 2011–2022.
18% mortality rate among hospitalized patients.
High-risk groups: older adults, immunocompromised individuals, pregnant women.

items.title: Outbreak Sources and Surveillance of Listeriosis
items.content: Major sources of listeriosis outbreaks in the U.S. have included hot dogs, deli meats, dairy products, and raw produce. PulseNet's use of whole-genome sequencing since 2013 has improved outbreak detection and investigation. Despite regulatory measures reducing ready-to-eat meat contamination, the overall incidence of listeriosis has not significantly declined.
keyPoints: Major sources: hot dogs, deli meats, dairy products, raw produce.
PulseNet transitioned to whole-genome sequencing in 2013.
Regulatory measures reduced ready-to-eat meat contamination but did not significantly lower listeriosis incidence.

items.title: Global Impact and Underrecognition of Listeriosis
items.content: Listeriosis is a significant global health issue, with outbreaks disproportionately affecting immunocompromised individuals and pregnant women. A large outbreak in South Africa highlighted the underrecognition of listeriosis in low- to middle-income countries, especially those with high HIV prevalence.
keyPoints: Significant global health problem.
Disproportionate impact on immunocompromised individuals and pregnant women.
Underrecognition in low- to middle-income countries with high HIV prevalence.

items.title: Incubation Period Variability in Listeria Infections
items.content: The incubation period for L. monocytogenes infections varies based on the type of disease and host factors, with shorter periods for gastroenteritis and longer for invasive diseases.
keyPoints: Average incubation <24 h for gastroenteritis, ~11 days for invasive disease
Longer incubation in pregnant women compared to nonpregnant adults

items.title: Characteristics and Mortality of Listeria Bacteremia
items.content: Bacteremia without a focus is the most common form of invasive listeriosis, with significant mortality influenced by several factors.
keyPoints: 3-month mortality rate for bacteremia: 46%
Risk factors for death include multiorgan failure, neoplasia, worsening comorbidities, monocytopenia (<200 cells/μL), older age, and female sex

items.title: Neurolisteriosis Presentation and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: Neurolisteriosis presents with symptoms similar to other bacterial meningitides but has distinct CSF findings, and MRI is preferred over CT for diagnosis.
keyPoints: 84% of neurolisteriosis cases present with meningoencephalitis
CSF white blood cell counts typically <1000/μL in 60–75% of patients
30-40% have low CSF glucose levels
3-month mortality rate: 30%
MRI superior to CT for diagnosing neurolisteriosis

items.title: Listeriosis in Pregnancy: Risks and Outcomes
items.content: Pregnant women are at increased risk for Listeria infection due to impaired cell-mediated immunity, with infections most common in the third trimester. While many infected pregnant women recover fully, a significant portion (~95%) do not have normal deliveries or postpartum courses. The majority of live-born infants from these pregnancies experience illness.
keyPoints: Pregnancy-associated listeriosis is most common in the third trimester.
~5% of infected pregnant women have a normal delivery and postpartum course.
Three-quarters of live-born infants are ill.
In a French cohort study: 24% fetal loss, 45% premature birth, 6% late-onset listeriosis in neonates, 21% term delivery with complications.
Fetal loss is uncommon after 29 weeks of gestation.

items.title: Neonatal Listeriosis: Early and Late-Onset Manifestations
items.content: Neonatal listeriosis can manifest as early-onset sepsis or late-onset meningitis. Early-onset sepsis, often associated with prematurity, is diagnosed within 48 hours after birth due to in utero infection. Late-onset meningitis occurs at ~2 weeks of age in full-term infants and results from postnatal acquisition.
keyPoints: Early-onset listeriosis typically diagnosed within 48 h after birth.
Late-onset listeriosis usually diagnosed at ~2 weeks of age in full-term infants.
70% of live-born infants developed early-onset listeriosis; 6% had late-onset listeriosis.
3% mortality rate among the 189 live-born infants.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Suspected Listeriosis
items.content: A high index of suspicion is crucial for timely diagnosis due to overlapping symptoms with other infections. Blood cultures are positive in about half the cases, and isolation from sterile sites confirms diagnosis.
keyPoints: Blood cultures positive ~50% of the time for suspected listeriosis in pregnant women
Isolation of L. monocytogenes from sterile sites (blood, CSF, amniotic fluid) is diagnostic
Selective enrichment media like PALCAM or Oxford Agar enhance organism isolation from nonsterile specimens
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.title: Use of Molecular and Advanced Techniques in Diagnosis
items.content: Commercial multiplex PCR panels for CSF can target L. monocytogenes, aiding diagnosis. MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry rapidly identifies the organism, while whole-genome sequencing helps solve outbreaks.
keyPoints: Multiplex PCR panels include L. monocytogenes as a diagnostic target
MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry provides rapid identification of L. monocytogenes
Whole-genome sequencing is useful for outbreak investigation
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.title: Guidelines on Antibiotic Therapy Duration for Listeriosis
items.content: The optimal duration of antibiotic therapy varies based on clinical syndrome and severity. Typical durations are 2 weeks for bacteremia, 3 weeks for meningitis, and up to 6-8 weeks for brain abscess or encephalitis.
keyPoints: Typical minimal treatment: 2 weeks for bacteremia, 3 weeks for meningitis
4–6 weeks for endocarditis; 6–8 weeks for brain abscess/encephalitis
Longer courses may be needed for immunocompromised patients or those not improving
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.title: Preventive Measures Against Listeriosis in High-Risk Individuals
items.content: Persons at increased risk for listeriosis should adhere to specific dietary precautions. This includes avoiding unpasteurized milk and soft cheeses, refrigerated pâté or meat spreads, premade deli salads, hot dogs, fermented or dry sausages, and ready-to-eat delicatessen foods unless they are heated to an internal temperature of 165°F.
keyPoints: Avoid unpasteurized dairy products
Avoid soft cheeses made with unpasteurized milk
Refrigerated pâté or meat spreads should be avoided
Premade deli salads like coleslaw, potato, tuna, or chicken salad should be avoided
Hot dogs and fermented/dry sausages should be heated to 165°F or until steaming hot

items.title: Hospital Dietary Precautions for Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Hospital dietary services must implement safe food-preparation procedures specifically for immunocompromised patients. These patients should not be served higher-risk foods to prevent listeriosis.
keyPoints: Implement safe food-preparation procedures in hospitals for immunocompromised patients
Avoid serving higher-risk foods to these patients

items.question: Which of the following best describes the growth characteristics of Listeria monocytogenes?
options: It is a nonsporulating, facultatively anaerobic gram-negative rod.
It grows optimally at 30–37°C and can grow as low as 4°C.
It requires high-pH environments to survive.
It cannot grow in refrigerated conditions.
items.explanation: Listeria monocytogenes is a nonsporulating, facultatively anaerobic gram-positive rod that grows optimally at 30–37°C and can also grow at refrigerator temperatures as low as 4°C.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which serotypes of Listeria monocytogenes are most commonly associated with human illness?
items.explanation: Nearly all human illness is caused by serotypes 1/2a, 1/2b, and 4b of Listeria monocytogenes.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which virulence factor of Listeria monocytogenes facilitates its escape from the internalization vacuole into the host cell cytosol?
items.explanation: The pore-forming cytolysin listeriolysin O (LLO) and phospholipases facilitate escape from the internalization vacuole into the cell cytosol, while internalins (InlA, InlB) promote invasion of host cells.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which cytokines play a key role in the innate immune response to L. monocytogenes infection?
items.explanation: Interferon $ \gamma $ and TNF-$ \alpha $ are key cytokines involved in the innate immune response to L. monocytogenes.

items.question: Which type of T cells is primarily responsible for long-term immunity against L. monocytogenes?
items.explanation: Cytotoxic (CD8+) T cells are the main contributors to long-term immunity against L. monocytogenes.

items.question: What is a significant risk factor for invasive listeriosis related to immune response?
items.explanation: Invasive listeriosis is associated with impaired cellular immunity.

items.question: When was foodborne transmission of L. monocytogenes first identified?
items.explanation: Foodborne transmission was not identified until over 50 years after L. monocytogenes was reported as a human pathogen, specifically in a 1983 outbreak investigation.

items.question: Which food product was linked to an outbreak of listeriosis resulting in eight deaths and the recall of over 30 million pounds of meat?
items.explanation: An outbreak linked to turkey deli meat resulted in eight deaths and the recall of over 30 million pounds of meat.

items.question: What is a major source of listeriosis outbreaks related to dairy products?
options: Soft cheese made with raw milk
items.explanation: Dairy products, especially soft cheese made with raw (unpasteurized) milk or produced from pasteurized milk in unsanitary facilities, are important sources of listeriosis outbreaks.

items.question: Which group is at the highest risk of L. monocytogenes infection?
items.explanation: Most people with invasive listeriosis are older adults, whose risk increases with each decade over 59 years of age.

items.question: What is the average incubation period for invasive listeriosis caused by L. monocytogenes?
items.explanation: The average incubation period for invasive disease is approximately 11 days.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms are major manifestations of acute febrile gastroenteritis caused by L. monocytogenes?
options: Fever, diarrhea, headache, and constitutional symptoms
Cerebral edema and high CSF glucose levels
Asymmetric cranial nerve palsies and ataxia
items.explanation: Major manifestations of acute febrile gastroenteritis include fever, diarrhea, headache, and constitutional symptoms.

items.question: What is the most common manifestation of invasive listeriosis?
items.explanation: Bacteremia without a focus is the most common manifestation of invasive listeriosis.

items.question: Which imaging modality is superior for diagnosing neurolisteriosis, including rhombencephalitis?
items.explanation: MRI is superior to CT for the diagnosis of neurolisteriosis, including rhombencephalitis.

items.question: Which trimester is pregnancy-associated listeriosis most commonly reported?
items.explanation: Pregnancy-associated listeriosis is most common in the third trimester.

items.question: What percentage of live-born infants develop early-onset listeriosis according to a recent update from the French cohort study?
items.explanation: According to the updated data, 70% of the 189 live-born infants developed early-onset listeriosis.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of granulomatosis infantiseptica?
options: Disseminated microabscesses and granulomas in the skin, liver, and spleen
items.explanation: Granulomatosis infantiseptica is characterized by disseminated microabscesses and granulomas in the skin, liver, and spleen.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.question: Which of the following specimens is NOT typically used for diagnosing invasive listeriosis?
items.explanation: Stool culture is not indicated in the evaluation of invasive listeriosis.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.question: What diagnostic method can rapidly identify an isolate as L. monocytogenes?
items.explanation: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectrometry can rapidly identify an isolate as L. monocytogenes.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.question: Which medium enhances the capacity for isolation of L. monocytogenes from nonsterile specimens?
options: Selective enrichment medium (e.g., PALCAM or Oxford Agar)
items.explanation: Selective enrichment medium enhances the capacity for isolation of L. monocytogenes from nonsterile specimens.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.question: What is a useful adjunct to culture in diagnosing listeriosis?
options: Commercially available multiplex PCR panels for CSF specimens
items.explanation: Commercially available multiplex polymerase chain reaction panels for CSF specimens include L. monocytogenes as a target and may be useful adjuncts to culture.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for individuals at increased risk for listeriosis?
options: Avoiding unpasteurized milk and soft cheeses made with unpasteurized milk.
Heating hot dogs until they reach an internal temperature of 165°F or are steaming hot.
Consuming refrigerated pâté or meat spreads without heating them.
Implementing safe food-preparation procedures in hospital dietary services for immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Individuals at increased risk should avoid consuming refrigerated pâté or meat spreads unless heated properly.

items.question: What is the recommended internal temperature for heating deli meats to prevent listeriosis?
items.explanation: Deli meats should be heated until they reach an internal temperature of 165°F or are steaming hot.

items.question: Which measure is NOT part of the general precautions to prevent listeriosis?
options: Taking TMP-SMX as prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii infection.
Cleaning hands, utensils, and kitchen surfaces after handling uncooked foods.
items.explanation: TMP-SMX is used as prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii infection, not a general precaution to prevent listeriosis.

items.statement: Febrile gastroenteritis caused by Listeria monocytogenes is commonly diagnosed through routine stool cultures.
items.explanation: Febrile gastroenteritis due to Listeria monocytogenes is rarely diagnosed because the organism is not detected by routine stool cultures or rapid diagnostic tests for stool specimens.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Iron-overload conditions, such as hemochromatosis, are associated with increased susceptibility to listeriosis.
items.explanation: Iron promotes the growth of Listeria monocytogenes in vitro, explaining why listeriosis is associated with iron-overload conditions like hemochromatosis.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · Listeria monocytogenes Infections | MICROBIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: L. monocytogenes infection can be more severe in patients treated with TNF- $ \alpha $ inhibitors.
items.explanation: The FDA added listeriosis to the boxed warning for TNF-$ \alpha $ inhibitors due to reports of invasive listeriosis.

items.statement: L. monocytogenes primarily induces a humoral immune response.
items.explanation: L. monocytogenes induces a vigorous cell-mediated immune response, not primarily a humoral one.

items.statement: The incidence of listeriosis has declined significantly since new regulations were implemented.
items.explanation: Although isolations from ready-to-eat meats decreased, the overall incidence of listeriosis has not declined significantly since the measures were implemented.

items.statement: Most patients with invasive listeriosis are children and adults without recognized risk factors.
items.explanation: Most people with invasive listeriosis have impaired cellular immunity or are older adults; only rarely do children and adults with no recognized risk factors develop the disease.

items.statement: The onset of neurologic disease in neurolisteriosis can be sudden or subacute.
items.explanation: Neurologic disease in neurolisteriosis can have a sudden or subacute onset.

items.statement: Most patients with neurolisteriosis present with CSF white blood cell counts greater than 1000/μL.
items.explanation: Most patients (~60–75%) have CSF white blood cell counts of <1000/μL.

items.statement: Neurolisteriosis and death are common in pregnant women without other risk factors.
items.explanation: Neurolisteriosis and death are rare in pregnant women without other risk factors.

items.statement: Most infants with granulomatosis infantiseptica survive after birth.
items.explanation: Most infants with granulomatosis infantiseptica are stillborn or die soon after birth.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.statement: Stool culture is indicated in the evaluation of invasive listeriosis.
items.explanation: Stool culture is not indicated in the evaluation of invasive listeriosis.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.statement: Cephalosporins are effective against L. monocytogenes.
items.explanation: Cephalosporins are not effective against L. monocytogenes.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.statement: Testing and treatment are indicated for an asymptomatic person who has eaten a product recalled because of L. monocytogenes contamination.
items.explanation: Testing and treatment are not indicated for asymptomatic individuals after consuming recalled products.

items.statement: Person-to-person transmission of listeriosis is common, requiring additional precautions beyond standard ones.
items.explanation: Person-to-person transmission of listeriosis is rare; only standard precautions are needed.

items.assertion: PulseNet transitioned to whole-genome sequencing in 2013.
items.reason: This transition improved the ability to detect and investigate outbreaks using laboratory-based surveillance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for why PulseNet's transition was significant.

items.assertion: Listeriosis outbreaks have been traced to hospital food.
items.reason: Hospitalized patients with immunocompromising conditions are often exposed to contaminated foods like sandwiches and ice cream.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why listeriosis outbreaks have been traced to hospital food.

items.assertion: Neurolisteriosis-associated mortality is higher among patients treated with dexamethasone.
items.reason: Dexamethasone may exacerbate the infection or interfere with immune response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason provided is not necessarily the correct explanation for higher mortality.

items.assertion: Rhombencephalitis disproportionately affects otherwise healthy older adults.
items.reason: Rhombencephalitis typically presents with respiratory failure in nearly half of the patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason given does not explain why rhombencephalitis disproportionately affects healthy older adults.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.assertion: A high index of suspicion can facilitate timely diagnosis of listeriosis.
items.reason: Symptoms of listeriosis overlap with those of other infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
items.assertion: Patients with neurolisteriosis routinely require follow-up lumbar puncture if they are improving clinically.
items.reason: Follow-up lumbar punctures are necessary to ensure the infection is fully resolved.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients with neurolisteriosis do not routinely require a follow-up lumbar puncture if they are improving.
items.reference: Listeria monocytogenes Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Infections Caused by Listeria monocytogenes
